content.text: Support Escalation Terms in Claude Contracts

Support escalation terms define how your team accesses Anthropic's engineering, safety, and account resources when something goes wrong in production. For enterprises, the gap between community-tier support (ticket queue, 48-hour response) and enterprise-tier support (named TAM, 30-minute P1 response) can be the difference between a 30-minute incident and a 4-hour outage. Negotiating the right support terms is as important as negotiating the SLA itself — because the SLA credit is the financial remedy for a breach that already happened, while the support terms determine how fast you resolve it.
A 99.95% availability SLA with no P1 support escalation is less useful than a 99.9% SLA with a dedicated TAM and a 30-minute war-room commitment. Resolution speed matters more than the credit amount.

attrs.alt: Claude enterprise support escalation tiers from self-service to executive escalation

content.text: Enterprise support programs for Claude API access are structured in four tiers with distinct access controls, response commitments, and use-case fit:
What to Negotiate in Each Tier
Business support is the floor for any production Claude deployment. Key negotiation points:
Production P1s don't wait for business hours
Without a named path, tickets stall
Needed for post-incident process and customer communication
Enterprise support is a contractual add-on that requires explicit negotiation. It is typically structured around three components:

A named Anthropic employee who is your primary technical contact, attends regular check-ins, maintains context on your architecture, and advocates internally for your issues. Negotiate: named individual (not 'a TAM from the pool'), minimum ½ day per week of dedicated time, 12-month commitment on TAM assignment continuity.

Your tickets bypass the standard queue and are seen by senior support engineers first. Negotiate: dedicated Slack Connect channel for real-time issue reporting (faster than ticket systems for P1s), named backup contact when your TAM is unavailable.

A committed phone or video call within 30 minutes of a P1 declaration. Negotiate: 24/7 coverage including weekends and holidays, a bridge number that connects directly (not through a call center), and an Anthropic engineer with API infrastructure context on the call — not just an account manager.
Executive escalation terms are typically available only for strategic accounts with significant annual contract values. They provide a bypass path when the standard support hierarchy has failed:
A VP or Director at Anthropic who is contractually identified as your executive sponsor, has authority to mobilize engineering resources, and commits to quarterly business reviews.

For P1 incidents exceeding 1 hour without resolution, Anthropic commits to initiating a war-room with: your technical lead, your executive sponsor or delegate, and senior infrastructure engineers. The war-room remains active until the incident is resolved or a mitigation is in place.

The DPA or support addendum should specify exactly when executive escalation is triggered — e.g., 'if a P1 incident exceeds 60 minutes without a resolution path identified, Customer may invoke executive escalation by [specific contact method].' Without a specific trigger, 'executive escalation' is a favor, not a commitment.
Safety and Policy Escalation — A Distinct Channel

Technical support escalation and safety/policy escalation are separate channels that must both be negotiated. Safety and policy escalations arise when: Claude's behavior violates your expected outputs in a way that may reflect a policy issue (not a technical bug), you receive a complaint from a user about Claude's response that requires Anthropic's review, or your use case is flagged during a usage audit and you need to discuss it with Anthropic's policy team.

A specific individual at Anthropic's Trust & Safety or Policy team who can review and respond to policy escalations within your organization. Without a named contact, policy questions route through the general support queue — with no defined response time.

For new use cases that may be novel or borderline, a committed policy review process: you submit a use case description, Anthropic responds with go/no-go and any required modifications within 10 business days. Prevents building for months and discovering a policy conflict at launch.

Require 60-day advance notice before Anthropic changes its AUP in ways that would affect your current production use cases. Default AUP changes can be effective immediately — 60-day notice gives you time to adapt.

If Claude's content filters block a legitimate request in your use case, a defined appeal process with a 5-business-day response window. Essential for regulated industries where over-refusal of certain content categories (medical, legal) creates compliance gaps.
Support terms are typically in a separate addendum to the master service agreement, not in the DPA or order form. The addendum should contain:

Support Terms Addendum — Key Sections

1. DEFINITIONS
   1.1 "P1 Incident": Complete inability to use the Claude API for production
       workloads, or API error rate > 50% sustained for > 5 minutes.
   1.2 "P2 Incident": Significant degradation; error rate 5-50% or TTFT P95
       > 3x baseline for > 15 minutes.
   1.3 "P3 Incident": Minor degradation; any issue not meeting P1/P2 criteria
       that affects production performance.
   1.4 "Business Hours": 9am–6pm in Customer's primary operating timezone,
       Monday–Friday, excluding public holidays.

2. SUPPORT CHANNELS
   2.1 Primary: Slack Connect channel #[customer]-anthropic-support
   2.2 P1 Escalation: [Direct phone number] — answered 24/7
   2.3 Policy Escalation: [Named contact email + backup]
   2.4 Executive Escalation: [Executive sponsor name, email, mobile]

3. RESPONSE TIME COMMITMENTS
   Priority  Initial Response    Update Frequency    Resolution Target
   P1        30 minutes, 24/7   Every 30 minutes    4 hours
   P2        2 hours, 24/7      Every 2 hours       24 hours
   P3        8 business hours   Daily               5 business days

4. POST-INCIDENT OBLIGATIONS
   4.1 Anthropic shall provide a written Root Cause Analysis (RCA) document
       within 5 business days of P1 resolution.
   4.2 RCA shall include: timeline of events, root cause, mitigation actions,
       and preventive measures planned.
   4.3 Customer may request a post-incident review call within 10 business
       days of any P1 incident.

5. DEDICATED TAM
   5.1 Assigned TAM: [Name, contact info — updated within 5 days of change]
   5.2 Backup TAM: [Name, contact info]
   5.3 Weekly 30-minute sync (reschedulable); quarterly business review
   5.4 TAM assignment change requires 30-day notice

6. EXECUTIVE ESCALATION TRIGGER
   6.1 Customer may invoke executive escalation if:
       (a) A P1 incident exceeds 60 minutes without a resolution path; OR
       (b) Three or more P2 incidents occur in any calendar month; OR
       (c) A material contract dispute arises that cannot be resolved at TAM level
   6.2 Executive response commitment: [Named executive] or delegate within
       15 minutes of invocation via [specific contact method]
Instrumenting Your Side of the Escalation Process
Support escalation only works if your team has the right information ready at the moment of escalation. Build a runbook that captures the data structure for P1 escalations:

# p1_incident_kit.py
# Data to gather before calling Anthropic P1 support line

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class P1IncidentKit:
    \"\"\"All data needed for a productive P1 escalation call.\"\"\"

    # Timeline
    first_error_timestamp: str       # ISO 8601 — when did errors start?
    detection_method: str            # monitoring alert / user report / PagerDuty

    # Scope
    error_rate_current: float        # % of requests returning errors (0.0–1.0)
    error_codes_seen: list[str]      # ["529", "503", "504"] etc.
    affected_models: list[str]       # ["claude-sonnet-5"] or ["all"]
    affected_regions: list[str]      # ["us-east-1"] or ["all"]
    affected_user_fraction: float    # % of end-users impacted

    # Recent changes
    last_deployment: Optional[str]   # timestamp of last code deploy
    config_changes: Optional[str]    # any recent config changes on your side

    # Reproduction
    sample_request_ids: list[str]    # 3–5 request IDs showing errors (from your logs)
    sample_curl: str                 # minimal curl that reproduces the error

    # Business impact
    revenue_impact_per_minute: Optional[float]  # USD/min if quantifiable
    customer_count_affected: Optional[int]

    # Status
    current_mitigation: str          # "fell back to cached responses" / "none"
    your_account_id: str             # Anthropic account ID
    contract_tier: str               # "enterprise" for priority routing

def generate_p1_brief(kit: P1IncidentKit) -> str:
    return f\"\"\"P1 ESCALATION BRIEF

Timeline: Errors started {kit.first_error_timestamp} | Detected via {kit.detection_method}
Error rate: {kit.error_rate_current:.1%} | Codes: {', '.join(kit.error_codes_seen)}
Affected: models={kit.affected_models}, regions={kit.affected_regions}
User impact: {kit.affected_user_fraction:.0%} of users
Current mitigation: {kit.current_mitigation}

Sample request IDs for log review:
{chr(10).join('  ' + r for r in kit.sample_request_ids)}

Account: {kit.your_account_id} | Tier: {kit.contract_tier}
\"\"\"

# Your on-call engineer completes this before the P1 call
kit = P1IncidentKit(
    first_error_timestamp="2026-07-01T23:42:00Z",
    detection_method="Datadog alert: error_rate > 10%",
    error_rate_current=0.35,
    error_codes_seen=["529", "503"],
    affected_models=["claude-sonnet-5"],
    affected_regions=["us-east-1"],
    affected_user_fraction=0.40,
    last_deployment="2026-07-01T22:15:00Z",
    config_changes=None,
    sample_request_ids=["msg_abc123", "msg_def456", "msg_ghi789"],
    sample_curl='curl https://api.anthropic.com/v1/messages -H "x-api-key: sk-..." ...',
    revenue_impact_per_minute=500,
    customer_count_affected=1200,
    current_mitigation="Fell back to cached responses for classification tasks",
    your_account_id="acct_xxxx",
    contract_tier="enterprise",
)
print(generate_p1_brief(kit))
Generic support queue; no advocate; context lost between tickets
Require named TAM as a contract deliverable, not a best-effort assignment
Extended degradation doesn't qualify as P1; standard response times apply
Include latency P95 breach and rate limit saturation in P1 definition
Escalation contact is a mailing list
24/7 mailing lists are rarely monitored at 3am
Require a direct mobile number for P1 escalation, not a shared inbox
Anthropic closes the ticket; you have no documentation for customers or internal learning
Contractually require RCA within 5 business days for P1 incidents
If your team is multi-lingual and incidents happen globally, language barriers slow escalation
Clarify language support availability; agree on English as the operational escalation language
Teams forget to submit; credits expire; Anthropic keeps the money
Negotiate automatic credit issuance; or at minimum a 90-day claim window with email reminder
Support SLA Benchmark Comparison by Provider

Before negotiating support terms with Anthropic, it helps to understand how Claude's offerings compare to those of other major AI API providers. The following benchmark table consolidates publicly available SLA information and common negotiated outcomes across the enterprise AI vendor landscape.

Use this table in vendor negotiations to anchor Anthropic's support commitments against competitive offerings. Where Anthropic's standard tier falls short of a competitor, cite the gap directly and request contract language that closes it.
Negotiating Service Credits and Remediation Rights

Service credits are the primary contractual remedy when a vendor misses an SLA. Anthropic's standard enterprise agreements define credit eligibility, but the credit percentages and claim procedures are frequently negotiated for large-spend customers.
Credits are typically calculated as a percentage of the monthly API fee for the affected service tier, prorated to the actual downtime window.
Most agreements require downtime to exceed 0.1% monthly (approximately 43 minutes) before credits are owed. Negotiate to lower or eliminate this threshold.
Standard agreements cap total monthly credits at 10–30% of monthly spend. Push for 30% minimum for production-critical workloads.
Vendors often prohibit stacking credits across simultaneous SLA breaches (uptime + latency on the same incident). Remove this restriction or negotiate separate credit pools.

Recommended Credit Claim Clause Language:

"Customer may submit a credit claim within thirty (30) calendar days
following the close of any calendar month in which a Service Level
Event occurred. Claims must include: (i) the date and duration of
the Service Level Event, (ii) the affected API endpoint(s), (iii)
Customer's internal incident ticket number, and (iv) sample API
error logs or latency traces. Anthropic will process and confirm or
dispute the claim within fifteen (15) business days. Credits not
disputed within thirty (30) days of issuance are deemed accepted.
Credits shall be applied to the subsequent invoice."

Key negotiation points:
- 30-day claim window (vs. vendors who require 5 business days)
- 15-day processing SLA on vendor side
- Undisputed credit auto-application without additional customer action

Enterprise customers with $500k+ annual commitments should also negotiate a right to request a Root Cause Analysis (RCA) document within 10 business days of any P1 incident, with the RCA explicitly included in the support contract addendum.

Negotiating support terms is only half the battle. Your team must be operationally prepared to invoke escalation paths, submit credit claims, and generate the documentation that substantiates SLA breach claims. This section covers the testing and monitoring patterns that make negotiated SLAs enforceable.
Verify all named contacts in the Anthropic console have been granted Enterprise support seat access (Settings → Support → Authorized Users).
Confirm the P1 emergency contact (phone/Slack/email) provided in the contract addendum is monitored 24×7 by your on-call rotation.
Run a test escalation at least 90 days before contract renewal — submit a non-urgent ticket via the agreed Enterprise channel and confirm SLA acknowledgment timing.
Set up automated latency monitoring against the baseline thresholds in your contract, with alerting that pages your on-call engineer before the SLA breach window closes.

Maintain a shared incident log template that captures: incident start time (UTC), first API error observed, first escalation action, Anthropic acknowledgment time, resolution time, and business impact estimate.
For safety-policy escalations, maintain a dedicated internal Slack channel (#ai-policy-escalation or equivalent) with the Anthropic trust-and-safety contact pinned.

import anthropic, statistics, time, datetime

def baseline_latency(n=50):
    """Measure p50/p95/p99 latency to use as SLA evidence baseline."""
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()
    latencies = []
    for _ in range(n):
        start = time.perf_counter()
        client.messages.create(
            model="claude-opus-4-8",
            max_tokens=64,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "ping"}],
        )
        latencies.append((time.perf_counter() - start) * 1000)
    s = sorted(latencies)
    return {
        "timestamp": datetime.datetime.utcnow().isoformat(),
        "n": n,
        "p50_ms": round(statistics.median(s), 1),
        "p95_ms": round(s[int(n * 0.95)], 1),
        "p99_ms": round(s[int(n * 0.99)], 1),
        "max_ms": round(max(s), 1),
    }

if __name__ == "__main__":
    import json
    result = baseline_latency()
    print(json.dumps(result, indent=2))
    # Save for SLA evidence: append to a JSONL file indexed by date

Run this baseline script weekly and store the output. When an SLA breach occurs, compare the breach-window latencies against your stored baseline to quantify the deviation — this is the evidence package your SLA credit claim requires.
Contract Renewal and SLA Ratchet Mechanisms

Enterprise support contracts are typically renewed annually. Well-structured contracts include SLA ratchet clauses that automatically tighten commitments if the vendor consistently exceeds their targets — rewarding high performance while ensuring continuous improvement.

"If Anthropic's actual monthly uptime exceeds the committed 99.9% for six (6) consecutive calendar months during the contract term, the Parties agree to negotiate in good faith an increase of the uptime commitment by 0.05 percentage points at the next annual renewal, up to a maximum commitment of 99.95%."

Mirror this mechanism for latency targets: if average P95 response time over a rolling 90 days is 20% better than the contracted baseline, negotiate a corresponding tightening of the contracted threshold at renewal.
Schedule an SLA performance review 60–90 days before contract expiration.
Request a vendor-provided performance report covering uptime, P95 latency, and support ticket resolution times for the full contract term.
Compare vendor data against your own internal monitoring — discrepancies may indicate measurement methodology differences worth resolving before signing a renewal.
Use the review to surface any incident patterns (e.g., repeated P2 issues in a specific region or API endpoint) that warrant new or tightened SLA language in the renewal.
If renewing with increased spend commitments, negotiate improved support tier access (e.g., upgrading from business to enterprise support) as part of the spend increase package.

Support escalation terms in Claude contracts should cover four tiers: self-service (all customers), business support (paid tiers, SLA-backed), enterprise support (dedicated TAM, 24/7 P1 bridge, priority queue), and executive escalation (named sponsor, war-room commitment). Negotiate the P1 definition to include sustained latency degradation and rate limit saturation — not just complete outages. Require a named TAM with a specific backup, a direct phone number for P1 escalation (not a mailing list), and a post-incident RCA commitment within 5 business days. Negotiate a safety and policy escalation channel separately from the technical support channel. Build an incident kit runbook so your on-call engineers have all required data ready before the escalation call — the first 60 seconds of a P1 call with Anthropic should be data transfer, not search.
